title: Rapid Response for Post-Op Day 1 Hemorrhagic Shock After Right Total Hip Arthroplasty: Massive Transfusion Protocol Activation, Team Role Assignment, and RN-vs-LVN-vs-CNA Scope During Acute Resuscitation
subtitle: RRT vs CODE BLUE — RRT for ACUTE DETERIORATION (Not Yet Arrested), CODE BLUE for ARREST (No Pulse/Apneic); BLOOD ADMINISTRATION + IV PUSH + VASOPRESSOR TITRATION + DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION = RN-ONLY (California LVN Cannot); 2-RN BLOOD VERIFICATION + Vital Signs q15 min During MTP + Watch for AHTR/TRALI/TACO + CITRATE Hypocalcemia; PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (SBP 80–90) Until Source Control; LETHAL TRIAD = Hypothermia + Acidosis + Coagulopathy (Warm Everything); SOURCE CONTROL via IR Embolization OR Return-to-OR Is the Life-Saving Intervention

patient.name: D. Hartley
patient.code: Full Code (POLST = Full Code; family at bedside; pre-op consent on file covers return to OR if needed)
patient.admitDate: Post-op Day 1 (R Total Hip Arthroplasty performed yesterday at 1300 for severe osteoarthritis); rapid response called by primary RN at 0915 for acute hypotension + tachycardia + altered mental status
patient.room: Ortho 6-West Bed 18 → rapid response team converging; OR + IR notified; ICU bed reserved

vitals.time: Post-op recovery yesterday afternoon (PACU discharge to floor)
vitals.temp: 36.8°C; pain 4/10 controlled; Hgb 11.4 (pre-op 13.2); est. intraop blood loss 450 mL; one peripheral 18g IV; on Lovenox 40 mg SQ q24h DVT prophylaxis (last dose 0830 today); abductor pillow in place; hip precautions

vitals.time: Day 1 0700 (routine AM VS by CNA — alert, oriented, normal)
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; pain 3/10 with PO oxycodone; AM CBC drawn (results pending); PT planned for 1000; reported feeling 'a little lightheaded with breakfast' to CNA who reported to RN

vitals.time: Day 1 0915 CURRENT (rapid response activation — sudden deterioration over 30 min)

labs.v: Primary RN found patient with new altered mental status (somnolent, mumbling, GCS 13 down from 15), pale + diaphoretic, cool extremities, BP 76/44, HR 138, RR 30, SpO2 91% on 2 L NC; large hematoma developing R thigh + groin (estimated 8 cm × 12 cm bulge — was not present at 0700); surgical dressing intact externally but underlying tissue swelling; abductor pillow in place; reports R thigh pain 10/10 (was 3/10 earlier); decreased urine output (only 50 mL in Foley over last 3 hr); peripheral pulses thready; cap refill 4 sec; RAPID RESPONSE TEAM called at 0915; charge RN + ICU outreach RN + respiratory therapist + intensivist converging; surgeon + anesthesia + interventional radiology + blood bank notified
labs.r: ACUTE HEMORRHAGIC SHOCK post-orthopedic surgery — likely deep wound/retroperitoneal bleed from surgical bed OR neurovascular bundle injury (common after hip arthroplasty: superior gluteal artery, profunda femoris branches). Class III–IV hemorrhagic shock (>30% blood volume loss with hypotension + tachycardia + altered mental status + cool/clammy + decreased UOP). RAPID RESPONSE activation: standing nurse-driven criteria empower the call. Differential also includes: anticoagulant-related bleeding (on Lovenox), pulmonary embolism (post-op risk — but hematoma + hemodynamics favor hemorrhage), septic shock (less likely without infection signs), cardiac (MI, tamponade — less likely without classic features). PRIORITY = hemorrhage control + resuscitation

labs.v: This is RAPID RESPONSE TEAM (RRT) activation, NOT a code blue — patient is hypotensive + tachypneic + altered but NOT pulseless, NOT apneic (still has palpable pulse + spontaneous respirations); if patient progresses to cardiopulmonary arrest (loses pulse OR becomes apneic with agonal respirations), then CODE BLUE activated + ACLS algorithm initiated; RRT criteria typically: HR <40 or >130, SBP <90 sustained, RR <8 or >28 sustained, SpO2 <90% despite O2, acute mental status change, new chest pain unrelieved, suspected stroke, anaphylaxis, seizure, worried staff/family — many institutions also allow family-activated RRT
labs.r: RRT = ACUTE DETERIORATION not yet arrested (proactive — buys time + brings ICU expertise to floor); CODE BLUE = ARREST (pulseless + apneic). DISTINCT teams + protocols. RRT typically: ICU outreach RN + respiratory therapist + intensivist/hospitalist + charge RN + primary RN. Code Blue adds: anesthesia, code cart, ACLS leader, more nurses. CRITICAL DIFFERENCE: RRT preserves an ARRESTABLE patient; Code Blue manages an ARRESTED one. NEVER wait for arrest to call — call EARLY

labs.n: TEAM ROLE ASSIGNMENT during RRT (charge RN coordinates)
labs.v:
ROLES assigned by charge RN within 60 seconds of arrival:
ROLE 1 — Primary RN (patient's nurse): rapid focused reassessment (ABCs, neuro, hematoma exam), IV push meds (when ordered), vasopressor initiation/titration once ordered; STAYS AT BEDSIDE
ROLE 2 — ICU Outreach RN: 2nd large-bore IV insertion (18g or 16g), arterial line assist, central line assist if placed, 2nd-RN blood verification + administration once blood arrives
ROLE 3 — Respiratory Therapist: airway management, escalating O2 (2 L NC → NRB → high-flow → BVM → intubation prep), ABG draw assist
ROLE 4 — Intensivist/Hospitalist: code/RRT leader, orders, source-control coordination (surgeon, IR), MTP activation, ICU disposition
ROLE 5 — Charge RN (you): runs the room, assigns roles, communicates with surgeon + IR + OR + blood bank + family, documents the timeline, monitors team performance, escalates per chain of command if needed
ROLE 6 — Recorder (LVN with focused VS + timeline documentation; OR designated code-cart RN): records vital signs q5 min during active resuscitation (q15 min once stabilized + during MTP), times all interventions, medication administration record, fluid/blood input + output
ROLE 7 — Runner (CNA): retrieves blood from blood bank, supplies from supply room, transports labs to lab, brings code cart, paged equipment, supports family communication; can do CHEST COMPRESSIONS if patient codes (BLS-trained); CANNOT push meds or interpret
labs.r: CLEAR ROLE ASSIGNMENT is the CORE skill of rapid response leadership. Without explicit role assignment, chaos ensues + critical tasks duplicated or missed. Charge RN/code leader assigns roles in the first 60 seconds. Roles match SCOPE: RN-only tasks (IV push, blood admin, vasopressor, interpretation, assessment of unstable) stay with RN; CNA + LVN handle scope-appropriate support tasks. NEVER assign someone outside their scope — even in an emergency. The FIVE RIGHTS OF DELEGATION still apply under pressure

labs.n: RN-ONLY tasks during this resuscitation (NOT delegable)
labs.v: RN-only: rapid focused assessment (ABCs, neuro, hematoma, perfusion), IV PUSH MEDS (e.g., fentanyl, vasopressor bolus, calcium gluconate, TXA if pushed rather than infused), BLOOD/BLOOD PRODUCT ADMINISTRATION + 2-RN verification + transfusion reaction monitoring, VASOPRESSOR INITIATION + TITRATION (norepinephrine, vasopressin, epinephrine drips), DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION (12-lead ECG, ABG, CBC, coags, lactate, ionized Ca trend, type & cross + crossmatch), CARE PLAN modification (resuscitation plan, transfer to ICU/OR/IR), TEACHING + FAMILY COMMUNICATION about diagnosis + plan, SBAR escalation to provider + chain of command, INFORMED CONSENT discussion if return-to-OR needed (provider obtains consent — RN witnesses + reinforces), DOCUMENTATION of resuscitation timeline + medications + outcomes
labs.r: RN scope boundary — these tasks cannot be delegated to LVN or CNA regardless of emergency. California-specific: LVNs CANNOT administer blood/blood products (BVNPT regulation); LVNs CANNOT push IV medications; LVNs CANNOT independently develop or modify care plan; LVNs CANNOT interpret diagnostic results; LVNs CANNOT do initial admission assessment or receive transfer report on unstable patient. The emergency doesn't change scope — it intensifies the need for clear role assignment WITHIN scope

labs.v: LVN can: serve as RECORDER (documentation of timeline, VS, interventions — within data collection scope; the RN reviews + signs care plan changes), administer SCHEDULED PO/IM/SQ medications on stable adjacent patients (reassign LVN's other-patient workload), HANG IV PIGGYBACK NS or LR (crystalloid resuscitation per cert — NOT blood, NOT IV push meds), provide FOCUSED VS reassessment q15 min during MTP (the RN interprets), perform WOUND/HEMATOMA INSPECTION + report findings to RN (no interpretation — just report), REINFORCE prior teaching to family (e.g., 'the team is working hard to stabilize her — surgeon is on the way'), MAINTAIN STERILE FIELD if needed for central line/A-line placement, assist with patient POSITIONING (Trendelenburg if ordered for hypotension — controversial, generally avoid; semi-Fowler for resp distress); CANNOT: push IV meds, hang blood, titrate vasopressors, interpret diagnostics, develop care plan, take report on unstable patient
labs.r: LVN scope during code/RRT — substantial support work but within scope. The LVN's RECORDER role is critical (accurate timeline = legal defense + quality improvement + handoff to ICU). LVN frees RN for IV push + blood admin + vasopressor + assessment. Always escalate to RN for any concerning changes. RN supervises + retains accountability

labs.v: CNA can: be the RUNNER (retrieve blood from blood bank with proper chain-of-custody slip — blood bank releases to anyone authorized to transport; CNA does NOT hang or verify blood; brings supplies, code cart, equipment, transports labs to lab), perform CHEST COMPRESSIONS if patient progresses to arrest (BLS-trained — depth 2–2.4 in adult, rate 100–120/min, full recoil, switch every 2 min to prevent fatigue), use AUTOMATED EXTERNAL DEFIBRILLATOR (AED) per BLS training (any BLS-trained provider can use AED; manual defibrillation requires ACLS-trained), MAINTAIN AIRWAY POSITIONING (head-tilt chin-lift if no spine concern), BAG-VALVE-MASK ventilation per BLS, COLLECT BASIC VITAL SIGNS (the RN/LVN interpret), assist with PATIENT POSITIONING + repositioning + transport, support FAMILY (escort to waiting area, provide comfort items, basic information per RN guidance — NOT clinical updates), provide POST-MORTEM CARE if outcome is death; CANNOT: push meds, hang blood, interpret, assess, modify care plan
labs.r: CNA scope during code/RRT — substantial support work plus BLS-trained tasks (compressions, BVM, AED). The runner role is critical (delays in getting blood from blood bank or supplies from storage = patient harm). CNA frees RN + LVN for higher-scope work. Always escalate to RN for any clinical questions. Scope does NOT change in emergency

labs.v: MTP ACTIVATION CRITERIA met: hemorrhagic shock with SBP <90 + HR >120 + active bleeding (visible hematoma + clinical deterioration); ABC score ≥2 (penetrating mechanism = 0 [no — surgical bleed], SBP <90 = 1, HR >120 = 1, FAST + = 0 [not done] → score 2); Shock Index 138/76 = 1.8 (>1 = shock); MTP ACTIVATED at 0920 (charge RN called blood bank: 'Activate MTP for 76F post-op hip arthroplasty with hemorrhagic shock; first cooler stat; type O-negative emergency-release until type-specific available; blood bank to deliver 1st MTP cooler within 10 min'); MTP COOLER 1: 6 units PRBC + 6 units FFP + 1 apheresis platelets (or 6 units pooled) = approximately 1:1:1 ratio (per trauma evidence — PROPPR trial); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1–2 g IV per 4 units PRBC (citrate in stored blood binds Ca → hypocalcemia → coagulopathy + arrhythmia); TXA (tranexamic acid) 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g IV over 8 hr (CRASH-2 trial — best within 3 hr of hemorrhage onset; surgical bleeding evidence less robust but commonly used); WARM ALL FLUIDS + BLOOD (Hotline or Level-1 rapid infuser); PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION target SBP 80–90 (MAP 60–65) until source controlled — avoid over-resuscitation diluting clotting factors + popping clot
labs.r: MTP = institutional protocol pre-staging large volumes of blood products in 1:1:1 ratio (PRBC:FFP:platelets) for ongoing hemorrhage. Best evidence from trauma (PROPPR trial); non-trauma use is widespread but less rigorously studied. KEY PRINCIPLES: (1) BALANCED RATIOS prevent dilutional coagulopathy from PRBC-only resuscitation, (2) PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (SBP 80–90) until source controlled, (3) CITRATE HYPOCALCEMIA from stored blood → replace Ca, (4) WARM EVERYTHING (lethal triad = hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy), (5) TXA early (within 3 hr) for major hemorrhage, (6) SOURCE CONTROL is the only definitive treatment — resuscitation buys time. Labs q15–30 min during MTP (CBC, coags, fibrinogen, ionized Ca, lactate, ABG)

labs.v: 2-RN BLOOD VERIFICATION required at bedside before each unit: (1) Patient identity — 2 identifiers (name + DOB or MRN) AGAINST patient's wristband AND blood product label, (2) Blood product type matches order (PRBC/FFP/platelets/cryoprecipitate), (3) ABO + Rh compatibility on label vs patient blood type (emergency-release O-neg = universal donor for women of childbearing age + unknown type — released without crossmatch when MTP active), (4) Unit number + expiration date + product integrity (no clots, no discoloration), (5) Both RNs sign verification; VITAL SIGNS at baseline + 15 min after start of each unit + at completion + q15 min during transfusion + 1 hr post; OBSERVE for ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR — fever, chills, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, hypotension, DIC; 1st 15 min are highest risk — RN STAYS AT BEDSIDE), TRALI (transfusion-related acute lung injury — new hypoxia + bilateral infiltrates within 6 hr of transfusion; non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema; supportive care), TACO (transfusion-associated circulatory overload — volume overload + pulmonary edema in patients with cardiac/renal compromise; diuretics + slow transfusion rate), FEBRILE NON-HEMOLYTIC (fever + chills without hemolysis — stop, acetaminophen, may resume slowly), ALLERGIC (hives → may premedicate + slow rate; anaphylaxis → STOP + EPI), BACTERIAL CONTAMINATION (rare; high fever + sepsis-like presentation); IF REACTION SUSPECTED: STOP transfusion, MAINTAIN IV with NS via NEW tubing, NOTIFY provider + blood bank, return blood + tubing + post-transfusion blood/urine samples to blood bank for workup
labs.r: BLOOD ADMINISTRATION is the highest-risk routine nursing task. 2-RN verification reduces wrong-blood-to-wrong-patient errors (a sentinel event with mortality ~10% for ABO-incompatible RBC). VS monitoring + bedside RN presence during first 15 min catches AHTR early. Distinguish reaction types: AHTR = STOP + treat aggressively (mortality high); TRALI = supportive (lungs); TACO = diuretics + slow (heart); febrile non-hemolytic = supportive (most common; may resume); allergic = antihistamine or EPI per severity. Document everything. Report to hemovigilance system

labs.n: SOURCE CONTROL — IR embolization vs return to OR
labs.v: Surgeon + interventional radiology consulted from RRT activation; bedside ultrasound by ICU outreach (FAST-like exam of thigh + groin) shows large hematoma with active extravasation suggested; HEMODYNAMICALLY MARGINAL after 2 units PRBC + 2 units FFP + 1 g TXA + 1 L LR (BP improving to 88/52 from 76/44; MAP 64; still tachycardic 124); options: (1) IR ANGIOGRAPHY + EMBOLIZATION of bleeding vessel (less invasive; faster; superior gluteal or profunda femoris branches commonly embolized — coil or gelfoam; works if hemodynamics can tolerate transport + IR availability), (2) RETURN TO OR for surgical exploration (definitive control; orthopedic surgeon + anesthesia; longer; required if IR fails or anatomy unfavorable); intensivist + surgeon + IR conference call at 0935; decision: IR FIRST given hemodynamic improvement (SBP >80 + responsive to resuscitation) — IR room ready in 10 min, anesthesia standing by, OR on standby if IR unsuccessful
labs.r: SOURCE CONTROL is the only definitive treatment for hemorrhagic shock — resuscitation only buys time. Post-arthroplasty bleeding source candidates: superior gluteal artery, profunda femoris branches, neurovascular bundle injury. IR angiography + embolization is increasingly first-line for post-surgical bleeds in hemodynamically borderline-stable patients (faster, less invasive, identifies + treats in one procedure). Return-to-OR if patient is too unstable for IR transport, IR fails, or anatomic complexity requires open exposure. Time-to-source-control is the most important modifiable factor

labs.n: LABORATORY profile (rapid response baseline + serial during MTP)
labs.v: BASELINE 0920: Hgb 6.8 (LOW — down from 11.4 yesterday → ~40% volume loss); Hct 21; Plt 124 (low end); INR 1.4 (slightly elevated — dilutional + on Lovenox); PTT 36; Fibrinogen 168 (low-normal); IONIZED CA 1.06 (low — pre-transfusion baseline); LACTATE 5.8 (elevated — tissue hypoperfusion); BMP: Na 138, K 4.2, Cl 102, HCO3 18 (low — metabolic acidosis), BUN 22, Cr 1.4 (mild AKI from hypoperfusion); GLUCOSE 142 (mild stress hyperglycemia); ABG pH 7.28 PaCO2 30 PaO2 92 on NRB (mixed metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation); type & crossmatch SENT (currently using O-neg emergency release until type-specific); SERIAL LABS during MTP q15–30 min: CBC, coags, fibrinogen, ionized Ca, lactate, ABG — guide ongoing replacement
labs.r: Hemorrhagic shock laboratory pattern: anemia (Hgb 6.8), thrombocytopenia (dilutional + consumption), coagulopathy (dilutional + consumption + hypothermia + acidosis), HYPOCALCEMIA (citrate in stored blood binds Ca → arrhythmia + coagulopathy risk), elevated lactate (tissue hypoperfusion), metabolic acidosis (lactic), AKI (pre-renal hypoperfusion + intrinsic injury). RESUSCITATION GOALS: Hgb >7 (>8 if cardiac), Plt >50 (>100 for CNS bleed), INR <1.5, fibrinogen >150–200 (cryoprecipitate if <150), ionized Ca >1.1, lactate clearance, MAP >65 (or permissive 60–65 until source control). Serial labs guide ongoing product/Ca/buffer replacement

labs.v: Temperature 35.6°C (NEW hypothermia — exposure during exam + cold IV fluids + cold blood products + cold OR transport history) — WARMING measures: Bair Hugger forced-air warmer covering torso + non-exposed extremities, WARM ALL FLUIDS + BLOOD via Hotline or Level-1 rapid infuser, warm humidified O2 via NRB → high-flow, increase room temperature, warm blankets, monitor temperature q15 min; ACIDOSIS (pH 7.28, lactate 5.8) addressed via: source control (stops further lactate production), volume resuscitation (improves perfusion), MTP (corrects hemorrhage-driven acidosis), AVOID excessive normal saline (chloride load → hyperchloremic acidosis — prefer balanced crystalloid LR or Plasma-Lyte), bicarbonate generally NOT given unless pH <7.1 (controversial); COAGULOPATHY addressed via: balanced MTP (1:1:1), TXA (1 g IV × 10 min then 1 g × 8 hr), warming (cold = coag dysfunction), source control, calcium replacement, fibrinogen/cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <150
labs.r: LETHAL TRIAD of trauma/hemorrhagic shock: hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy. Each one worsens the others (cold platelets don't work; acidotic platelets don't work; coagulopathic bleeding causes more shock causing more acidosis). PREVENTION + REVERSAL is part of the resuscitation strategy: WARM EVERYTHING, treat acidosis at the source (perfusion + product not bicarb), correct coagulopathy with balanced products + TXA + Ca. Avoid common errors: rapid crystalloid (hyperchloremic acidosis from NS), unwarmed products (hypothermia), PRBC-only resuscitation (dilutional coagulopathy)

labs.v: Husband + adult daughter at bedside when RRT called; family escorted by CNA to private family room within unit (not removed from facility — accessible for updates); designated FAMILY LIAISON: charge RN or social worker provides updates every 15–30 min during resuscitation (without leaving bedside untended — coordinate timing); information shared: 'D. is having a serious complication from yesterday's surgery — bleeding. The team is working as fast as possible to stabilize her with blood products and to find and stop the source of bleeding. The surgeon and interventional radiology are on the way. Once she is stable enough we will move her to the special radiology suite to find and stop the bleeding. I will come back to update you in 15 minutes'; FAMILY PRESENCE DURING RESUSCITATION (FPDR): institutional policy varies — increasingly endorsed by AACN/ENA when family wishes to be present + supported by a dedicated family-support staff member (chaplain, social work, designated nurse); CHAPLAIN + SOCIAL WORK paged; UPDATES on disposition (IR vs OR vs ICU); INFORMED CONSENT discussion for return-to-OR/IR conducted by provider (surgeon/intensivist) — RN supports + witnesses; emergency exception applies if patient unable to consent + family unavailable + life-threatening: provider may proceed under emergency doctrine, document thoroughly
labs.r: FAMILY COMMUNICATION during code/RRT is RN/charge RN/code leader responsibility (NOT delegable to CNA for clinical updates — CNA can escort + provide comfort items + basic non-clinical information). Family presence during resuscitation (FPDR) is increasingly supported when properly facilitated. Designated family liaison ensures consistent updates without distracting the team at the bedside. Honest, simple language. Frequent updates. Document family communication. Chaplain + social work activation. Informed consent for procedures = provider obtains, RN witnesses + reinforces

labs.v: RECORDER (LVN or designated RN) documents in real-time: timeline of events (when RRT called, who arrived, role assignments, when MTP activated, every intervention with time), VS q5 min during active resuscitation (q15 min during MTP/stabilization), all medications + blood products with unit numbers + dose + route + time, fluid input + output, lab results as they return, source-control decisions + rationale, family communication + presence, provider notifications + responses, escalations (chain of command if any); HANDOFF to IR/OR team via SBAR: Situation (76F POD1 R THA with hemorrhagic shock from likely deep wound bleed), Background (medical history, surgery, pre-op labs, intraop course, post-op course, current resuscitation, products given so far), Assessment (current VS, mental status, hemodynamic trajectory, lab trends, source-control status, pending), Recommendation (transfer to IR for embolization with OR standing by; MTP ongoing; ICU bed reserved post-procedure); HANDOFF includes: chart, MAR, all labs, RRT timeline documentation, family contact info, code status (full code), allergies, weight, height, last meal/anesthesia history, current IV access, current drips, blood products given + pending, hemodynamic + ventilatory parameters
labs.r: DOCUMENTATION = legal record + quality improvement + clinical handoff. The RECORDER role is critical — without accurate timeline, all later analysis (M&M conference, sentinel event review, malpractice defense, ICU continuity) is compromised. SBAR handoff is the standard for transitions of care. Include all relevant clinical + administrative data. NEVER let documentation prevent care — but always reconstruct timeline immediately after stabilization if real-time documentation impossible. The LVN's recorder role + the runner's task tracking + the RN's verbal updates all feed into the documented record

labs.v: No chest pain (rules out concurrent MI as primary driver); no calf swelling/tenderness or sudden dyspnea/pleuritic chest pain (PE less likely vs hemorrhage given hematoma); no fever or signs of infection (sepsis less likely); no head injury/fall (rules out trauma); no anticoagulant overdose history beyond Lovenox prophylaxis (last dose 0830 — 30 mg SQ; reversal with andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC NOT typically used for prophylactic dose); no known coagulopathy or hemophilia; no NSAID use in past 48 hr beyond intraop ketorolac (1 dose); no aspirin use; no jehovah's witness status (would change consent process); pre-op consent on file covers return to OR if needed for surgical complications
labs.r: Rules out alternative drivers + confirms hemorrhage as primary mechanism. Lovenox prophylactic dose less concerning than therapeutic dose but still affects bleeding — surgery + IR teams aware. Note pre-op consent: orthopedic surgical consent typically covers 'return to OR for surgical complications' — but if extensive new procedure planned (e.g., amputation), separate consent may be needed. Provider obtains consent; RN witnesses

nursesNote: 0915 — 76F POD1 R Total Hip Arthroplasty (yesterday 1300) developed acute hemorrhagic shock with new altered mental status, hypotension (76/44 from 122/74), tachycardia (138 from 88), tachypnea (30 from 16), hypoxia (91% on 2L NC), hypothermia (35.6°C — new), and large developing hematoma R thigh/groin (8 cm × 12 cm bulge, surgical dressing intact externally). Primary RN activated RAPID RESPONSE TEAM at 0915. As CHARGE RN responding, I assumed RRT leadership coordination + assigned roles within 60 seconds: ROLE 1 Primary RN (patient's nurse) — focused reassessment + IV push meds + vasopressor titration once ordered, STAYS AT BEDSIDE; ROLE 2 ICU Outreach RN — 2nd large-bore 18g IV placed, assisting with arterial line, will serve as 2nd-RN verifier for blood; ROLE 3 Respiratory Therapist — escalated O2 to NRB 15 L (SpO2 → 96%), preparing for high-flow/intubation if needed; ROLE 4 Intensivist (Dr. Patel) — code/RRT leader, orders, source-control coordination; ROLE 5 Charge RN (me) — running room, coordinating with surgeon + IR + blood bank + family + documenting + escalating; ROLE 6 Recorder LVN — real-time timeline + VS q5 min + medication MAR; ROLE 7 Runner CNA — retrieving MTP cooler from blood bank, supplies, transporting labs, supporting family. ASSESSMENT: Class III-IV hemorrhagic shock from likely deep wound/retroperitoneal bleed (superior gluteal or profunda femoris branches — common post-THA neurovascular bundle injury); on Lovenox 40 mg SQ prophylaxis (last dose 0830); pre-op consent on file covers return to OR. RRT vs CODE BLUE distinction: this is RRT (acute deterioration not yet arrested) — patient still has pulse + spontaneous respirations + responsiveness; if progresses to arrest, will activate code blue + ACLS. MTP ACTIVATED at 0920 (called blood bank: 'Activate MTP for 76F POD1 hip arthroplasty with hemorrhagic shock; first cooler stat; O-neg emergency release until type-specific available'); MTP COOLER 1 arrived 0928: 6 units PRBC + 6 units FFP + 1 apheresis platelets (~1:1:1 ratio per PROPPR trial); 2-RN BLOOD VERIFICATION at bedside (patient identifiers x2 against wristband + blood product label, ABO/Rh compatibility, unit numbers, expiration dates, product integrity, both RNs sign); first PRBC unit started at 0932 via Level-1 rapid infuser (WARMED to prevent hypothermia compounding); transfusion reaction surveillance (VS at baseline + 15 min after start + completion + q15 min during MTP; bedside RN x first 15 min of each unit for AHTR detection); CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1 g IV given (citrate hypocalcemia prevention — 1-2 g per 4 units PRBC; ionized Ca baseline 1.06 — low); TXA 1 g IV over 10 min started + 1 g over 8 hr scheduled (within 3 hr of hemorrhage onset per CRASH-2); PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION target SBP 80-90 / MAP 60-65 until source control (avoid over-resuscitation diluting clotting factors + popping clot); LR + Plasma-Lyte preferred over NS (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis); WARMING measures (Bair Hugger forced-air warmer torso + extremities, warm IV/blood via Hotline, warm humidified O2, increase room temp, warm blankets, temp q15 min) — preventing LETHAL TRIAD (hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy). LABS baseline 0920: Hgb 6.8 (down from 11.4 yesterday = ~40% volume loss), Plt 124, INR 1.4, Fibrinogen 168, ionized Ca 1.06, Lactate 5.8, ABG pH 7.28/PaCO2 30/PaO2 92 on NRB (mixed metabolic acidosis); serial labs q15-30 min during MTP. SOURCE CONTROL: surgeon + IR conferenced at 0935 — bedside US shows large hematoma with suggested active extravasation; hemodynamics improving with resuscitation (BP 88/52, MAP 64 from 76/44, HR 124 from 138 after 2 units PRBC + 2 units FFP + TXA + 1 L LR); IR EMBOLIZATION selected as first-line (faster, less invasive, identifies + treats; superior gluteal/profunda femoris branches commonly embolized via coil or gelfoam); IR room ready in 10 min; anesthesia standing by; OR on standby if IR unsuccessful; ICU bed reserved post-procedure. RN-only tasks retained: assessment, IV push, blood admin + verification + transfusion reaction monitoring, vasopressor titration (norepinephrine started 0.05 mcg/kg/min via primary RN once second IV established + central access pending), diagnostic interpretation, care plan modification, SBAR escalation, informed consent witness (provider obtains). LVN scope: recorder role (timeline, MAR, VS q15 min during MTP, fluid I&O), focused VS reassessment (RN interprets), hematoma inspection + report (no interpretation), reinforce family teaching, maintain sterile field for line placement, position patient. CNA scope: runner (blood retrieval with chain-of-custody slip from blood bank, supplies, code cart, transport labs), family escort + comfort + non-clinical information per RN guidance, BLS-trained for compressions/BVM/AED if patient codes; cannot push meds, hang blood, interpret. FAMILY COMMUNICATION: husband + adult daughter escorted by CNA to private family room within unit (accessible for updates — not removed from facility); designated family liaison (charge RN) provides updates every 15-30 min in honest simple language; chaplain + social work paged; updates on disposition (IR → ICU); informed consent for IR embolization obtained by surgeon/IR attending (RN witnesses + reinforces); family presence during resuscitation policy per institution — family declined to be at bedside during active resuscitation, preferring private room with updates. ESCALATION via SBAR throughout: Situation (POD1 hip arthroplasty in hemorrhagic shock), Background (76F, OA, yesterday's surgery, no anticoag beyond prophylactic Lovenox, NKDA, full code), Assessment (Class III-IV shock from likely deep wound bleed, responsive to resuscitation, improving with MTP, going to IR), Recommendation (continue MTP, IR embolization, OR standby, ICU bed reserved); CHAIN OF COMMAND not needed — providers responsive throughout. ANTICIPATED IR procedure: angiography to identify bleeding vessel + selective embolization with coil or gelfoam; if IR unsuccessful or anatomy unfavorable, return to OR for open surgical exploration; if profound shock recurs or patient codes, activate Code Blue + ACLS + emergent open exploration. POST-IR/POST-OR DISPOSITION: ICU for continued resuscitation, hemodynamic monitoring (arterial + central lines), MTP continuation as needed, serial labs (CBC + coags + fibrinogen + ionized Ca + lactate + ABG q4-6h), warming continued, source-control reassessment, family communication, anticipate prolonged ICU course (1-3 days) + step-down then floor + rehab; complications to monitor: ongoing/recurrent bleed, transfusion reactions (AHTR/TRALI/TACO), AKI (hypoperfusion + contrast from IR), DVT/PE (paradoxical risk despite recent bleed — anticoag decision balanced with bleed risk; mechanical prophylaxis SCDs only initially; pharmacologic when safe), surgical site infection, delirium (especially in 76 y/o with hospital + ICU course), pressure injury (hemodynamic instability + prolonged immobility), reconditioning + PT/OT, eventual goal of return to functional independence. DOCUMENTATION: real-time timeline of RRT, MTP activation, blood products with unit numbers + 2-RN verification, all medications + doses + times, fluid I/O, lab trends, source-control decisions + rationale, family communication + presence, provider notifications + responses, SBAR handoff to IR. DEBRIEF planned for end of resuscitation + end of shift to review priorities, team performance, delegation effectiveness, system processes (was MTP delivery timely? communication clear? roles understood? family well-supported?), any close-call learning, quality improvement opportunities, emotional support for team (high-acuity events take a toll — EAP + peer support offered).
providerOrders: ACTIVATE RAPID RESPONSE TEAM (already called by primary RN — charge RN coordinates response)

TEAM ROLE ASSIGNMENT (charge RN assigns within 60 seconds of arrival): Primary RN at bedside (assessment + IV push + vasopressor), ICU Outreach RN (2nd IV + 2nd-RN blood verifier), Respiratory Therapist (airway/O2/ABG), Intensivist (code leader + orders + source-control coordination), Charge RN (room runner + family + blood bank + chain of command + documentation oversight), Recorder LVN (real-time timeline + VS + MAR), Runner CNA (blood retrieval + supplies + transport + family escort + BLS-ready)
ESCALATE TO CODE BLUE if patient progresses to arrest (loss of pulse OR apnea with agonal respirations) — activate ACLS algorithm
AIRWAY + BREATHING:
Escalate O2: 2 L NC → NRB 15 L/min → high-flow NC 40-60 L/min FiO2 0.5-1.0 → BVM if apneic → intubation prep if mental status declining or respiratory failure
Anesthesia paged for intubation if needed; RSI medications prepped (etomidate or ketamine for induction in shock — AVOID propofol in hypotension; succinylcholine or rocuronium for paralysis)
ABG draw + analysis (RT assists; RN interprets)
CIRCULATION + HEMORRHAGE CONTROL:
2nd large-bore IV (18g or 16g) for volume + blood products (ICU outreach RN places)
ARTERIAL LINE (radial or femoral) for continuous BP monitoring during MTP + vasopressor titration
CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETER (subclavian or internal jugular) for vasopressor + central access (provider places; RN assists)
FOLEY catheter monitoring (already in place from surgery) — goal UOP ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr (currently dropping to 50 mL/3 hr = ~0.2 mL/kg/hr — poor perfusion marker)
MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (MTP) ACTIVATION:

Charge RN calls blood bank: 'Activate MTP for [patient name + MRN] — hemorrhagic shock from post-op hip arthroplasty bleed; first cooler stat; O-negative emergency release until type-specific available'
MTP COOLER (institution-specific): typically 6 units PRBC + 6 units FFP + 1 apheresis platelets (or 6 pooled) per cooler ~1:1:1 ratio (per PROPPR trial)
Type & crossmatch SENT immediately on activation (transition to type-specific products when crossmatched)
2-RN BLOOD VERIFICATION at bedside before each unit: 2 patient identifiers vs wristband vs blood label, ABO/Rh compatibility, unit number, expiration, product integrity; both RNs sign
Administer via Level-1 rapid infuser (WARMED — prevents hypothermia compounding); large-bore IV (18g minimum for blood)
VS at baseline + 15 min after start of each unit + completion + q15 min throughout MTP + 1 hr post-completion (transfusion reaction surveillance)

TRANSFUSION REACTION surveillance: AHTR (fever, chills, flank pain, hemoglobinuria, hypotension, DIC — STOP + treat aggressively), TRALI (new hypoxia + bilateral infiltrates within 6 hr — supportive), TACO (volume overload + pulmonary edema in cardiac/renal compromise — diuretics + slow rate), febrile non-hemolytic (most common — acetaminophen + may resume slowly), allergic (hives → antihistamine + slow; anaphylaxis → STOP + EPI), bacterial contamination (rare; sepsis-like)

IF REACTION SUSPECTED: STOP transfusion, MAINTAIN IV with NS via NEW tubing, NOTIFY provider + blood bank, return blood + tubing + post-transfusion blood/urine samples to blood bank for workup, document in chart + hemovigilance
CALCIUM REPLACEMENT (citrate hypocalcemia prevention):
CALCIUM GLUCONATE 1-2 g IV per 4 units PRBC transfused (ionized Ca q15-30 min during MTP — target >1.1 mmol/L)
OR Calcium chloride (3x more elemental Ca than gluconate but more vesicant — needs central line)
TXA (TRANEXAMIC ACID) — antifibrinolytic per CRASH-2:
TXA 1 g IV over 10 min, then 1 g IV over 8 hr
Best within 3 hr of hemorrhage onset (later use less effective; possibly harmful >3 hr per CRASH-2)
PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION strategy (until source control):
Target SBP 80-90, MAP 60-65 until bleeding controlled (avoid over-resuscitation that dilutes clotting factors + pops clot)
Once source controlled (post-embolization or post-surgical control): standard targets MAP ≥65, normalize hemodynamics
BALANCED CRYSTALLOID (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis from NS):
Lactated Ringer's (LR) or Plasma-Lyte preferred; warmed
Limit crystalloid in favor of blood products in hemorrhagic shock (over-crystalloid worsens dilutional coagulopathy)
VASOPRESSORS (if shock persists despite adequate volume + blood resuscitation — caution: hemorrhage requires VOLUME not pressors as primary, but bridge to source control):
NOREPINEPHRINE 0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min titrated to MAP target (permissive 60-65 until source control; ≥65 after)
RN-only initiation + titration; central line preferred (peripheral acceptable in emergency)
WARMING (LETHAL TRIAD prevention — hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy):
Bair Hugger forced-air warmer (torso + non-exposed extremities)
Warm all IV fluids + blood products via Hotline or Level-1 rapid infuser
Warm humidified O2 via NRB → high-flow
Increase room temperature; warm blankets; temperature q15 min
SERIAL LABORATORY monitoring (q15-30 min during MTP):
CBC (Hgb target >7; >8 if cardiac/cerebral)
Coag panel (INR target <1.5; PTT target normal)
Fibrinogen (target >150; >200 in some protocols — cryoprecipitate if <150-200)
Ionized calcium (target >1.1; replace with calcium gluconate or chloride)
Lactate (clearance is resuscitation marker; rising = ongoing hypoperfusion)
ABG (acid-base; PaO2/FiO2 for ARDS risk)
Potassium (rises with PRBC transfusion + cell lysis — monitor; replace if symptomatic hypokalemia or treat hyperkalemia)
Type & crossmatch (transition to type-specific products)
SOURCE CONTROL coordination (charge RN/intensivist):
STAT surgeon + interventional radiology (IR) consult; intensivist + surgeon + IR conference for decision
Option 1: IR ANGIOGRAPHY + EMBOLIZATION (faster, less invasive; superior gluteal/profunda femoris branches commonly embolized; works if hemodynamically transportable)
Option 2: RETURN TO OR for surgical exploration (definitive control; required if IR fails or unfavorable anatomy or too unstable for IR transport)
Bedside ultrasound for hematoma assessment if rapidly available + hemodynamics allow
CT angiography if hemodynamically stable + IR planned (often deferred — IR can do diagnostic angiography in same procedure)
FAMILY COMMUNICATION + SUPPORT:
Husband + adult daughter escorted to private family room within unit (accessible for updates, not removed from facility)
Designated FAMILY LIAISON (charge RN or social worker) provides updates every 15-30 min in honest simple language
CHAPLAIN + SOCIAL WORK paged
Family presence during resuscitation (FPDR) per institutional policy + family preference + dedicated support staff facilitation

INFORMED CONSENT for IR/OR procedures obtained by provider (surgeon/IR attending); RN witnesses + reinforces; emergency exception applies if patient unable to consent + family unavailable + life-threatening
DOCUMENTATION (real-time recorder + post-event reconstruction if needed):
Timeline (RRT call, arrivals, role assignments, MTP activation, every intervention with time)
VS q5 min during active resuscitation, q15 min during MTP/stabilization
All medications + doses + routes + times (MAR)
All blood products with unit numbers + 2-RN verification + reaction surveillance
Fluid I&O
Lab results as they return
Source-control decisions + rationale
Family communication + presence
Provider notifications + responses
Escalations (chain of command if any)
SBAR HANDOFF to IR team + ICU team upon transfer:
Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation

Include: chart, MAR, all labs, RRT timeline, family contact, code status, allergies, weight, height, last meal/anesthesia, current IV access + drips, blood products given + pending, hemodynamic + ventilatory parameters
POST-PROCEDURE ICU DISPOSITION (after IR or OR):
Continued hemodynamic monitoring (arterial + central lines)
MTP continuation as needed; serial labs q4-6h once stable
Warming continued; source-control reassessment; transfusion reaction surveillance continued (delayed AHTR possible)
AKI monitoring (CRRT consideration if progresses)
DVT/PE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) initially given bleed; pharmacologic when safe (typically 24-48 hr post bleeding controlled + stable hemodynamics)
Surgical site infection surveillance
Delirium prevention + assessment (CAM-ICU)
Pressure injury prevention (frequent repositioning, pressure-redistribution surface)
Early mobility once stable
Nutritional support (NPO initially; advance when stable)
Family communication + multidisciplinary rounding
DEBRIEFING after event (end of resuscitation + end of shift):
Review priorities + team performance + delegation effectiveness
System process review (MTP delivery time, communication, role clarity, family support, equipment availability)
Quality improvement opportunities + sentinel event review if criteria met
Emotional support for team (EAP + peer support; high-acuity events take emotional toll)
Documentation of debrief findings + action items

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters right now at the RRT call?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring immediate RRT attention.

opts.text: Acute hemorrhagic shock: BP 76/44 (dropped from 122/74), HR 138, RR 30, SpO2 91% on 2 L NC, new altered mental status (GCS 13), pale + diaphoretic + cool extremities, cap refill 4 sec, UOP 50 mL/3 hr
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Class III-IV hemorrhagic shock (>30% blood volume loss with hypotension + tachycardia + altered mental status + cool/clammy + decreased UOP). Time-critical. Source control + resuscitation simultaneously. MTP activation.

opts.text: New large hematoma R thigh/groin (8 cm × 12 cm bulge — was not present at 0700); pain 10/10 (was 3/10 earlier); surgical dressing intact externally but underlying tissue swelling
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — visible bleeding source. Post-THA neurovascular bundle injury (superior gluteal artery, profunda femoris branches commonly involved). Identifies source for IR/surgical intervention. Pain change is a sentinel sign of new pathology.

opts.text: RRT vs CODE BLUE distinction — this is RRT (patient still has pulse + spontaneous respirations + responsiveness, even if deteriorating); CODE BLUE only if progresses to arrest (pulseless + apneic/agonal)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — correct activation. RRT = ACUTE DETERIORATION not yet arrested (buys time + brings ICU expertise). Code Blue = ARREST (ACLS). Distinct protocols. NEVER wait for arrest to call — call early. Family/staff-activated RRT also typical.

opts.text: Hypothermia 35.6°C (new — exposure during exam + planned cold fluids/blood); LETHAL TRIAD prevention requires warming (hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — lethal triad prevention. Bair Hugger + warm all fluids/blood via rapid infuser + warm humidified O2 + room temp + warm blankets + temp q15 min. Cold platelets don't work; cold = coagulopathy = worse bleeding.

opts.text: TEAM ROLE ASSIGNMENT needed within 60 seconds: Primary RN (bedside), ICU Outreach RN (2nd IV + blood verifier), RT (airway), Intensivist (orders), Charge RN (coordinator), Recorder LVN (timeline), Runner CNA (blood retrieval + supplies); EACH ROLE MATCHED TO SCOPE
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — clear role assignment is the CORE skill of RRT leadership. Without it, chaos + duplicated/missed tasks. Five Rights of Delegation still apply under pressure. Match each role to scope (LVN can't push meds; CNA can't interpret).

opts.text: MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (MTP) ACTIVATION criteria met: SBP <90 + HR >120 + active hemorrhage; Shock Index 1.8 (>1 = shock); ABC score ≥2 (penetrating=0, SBP<90=1, HR>120=1); first MTP cooler stat with O-neg emergency release
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — MTP activation criteria + workflow. Balanced 1:1:1 (PRBC:FFP:plt) per PROPPR trial. O-neg until type-specific. Calcium replacement (citrate hypocalcemia). TXA (CRASH-2 — within 3 hr). Permissive hypotension until source control. Warming. Serial labs.

opts.text: RN-only tasks: rapid focused assessment, IV push meds, BLOOD ADMINISTRATION + 2-RN verification + transfusion reaction monitoring, VASOPRESSOR titration, diagnostic interpretation, care plan modification, SBAR escalation; CANNOT delegate even in emergency
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — RN scope boundaries. California LVN CANNOT push IV meds, hang blood/blood products, titrate vasopressors, interpret diagnostics, develop care plan, receive transfer report on unstable patient. Emergency doesn't change scope — it INTENSIFIES need for clear role assignment within scope.

opts.text: SOURCE CONTROL is the only definitive treatment — resuscitation only buys time; surgeon + IR consult immediately; options: IR angiography + embolization (less invasive, faster) OR return-to-OR (definitive, required if IR fails or unstable for transport)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — source control prioritization. Time-to-source-control is the most important modifiable factor in hemorrhagic shock. IR first-line if hemodynamically tolerable (most post-arthroplasty bleeds can be embolized — superior gluteal, profunda femoris branches). OR if too unstable or IR fails.

opts.text: FAMILY COMMUNICATION during resuscitation: husband + adult daughter escorted to private family room within unit (accessible for updates — not removed from facility); designated family liaison provides honest updates every 15-30 min; chaplain + social work paged; informed consent for procedures obtained by provider (RN witnesses)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — family-centered care during code/RRT. Designated liaison ensures consistent updates without distracting bedside team. Honest simple language. Chaplain + social work activation. Family presence during resuscitation (FPDR) per institutional policy + family preference.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION (RECORDER role) in real-time: timeline of events, role assignments, MTP activation, every intervention with time, VS q5 min during active resuscitation, all medications + blood products with unit numbers + 2-RN verification, lab trends, source-control decisions, family communication, provider notifications
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — documentation = legal record + quality improvement + clinical handoff. Recorder role critical (without accurate timeline, all later analysis compromised). SBAR handoff to IR/ICU. LVN can serve as recorder; RN reviews + signs care plan elements.

opts.text: Patient was 'a little lightheaded with breakfast' at 0800 per CNA report to RN — CNA appropriately escalated change in patient status without interpretation
opts.rat: Appropriate CNA escalation — early warning sign that was reported to RN per Right Direction/Communication principle (CNA does not interpret but reports out-of-norm observations). The RN should have escalated assessment at that time. Lessons-learned for debrief, but not a current intervention priority.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA
opts.rat: Reassuring — no documented allergies; document and proceed. No medication allergies to consider in resuscitation drug selection. Note for any antibiotic selection if needed.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What scope, delegation, and resuscitation principles apply?
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying scope/delegation/RRT/MTP principles.

opts.text: RRT vs CODE BLUE distinction: RRT = ACUTE DETERIORATION not yet arrested (proactive — buys time, brings ICU expertise to floor); CODE BLUE = ARREST (pulseless + apneic/agonal — ACLS algorithm); distinct teams + protocols; never wait for arrest to call
opts.rat: CORRECT — proper distinction + activation principles. RRT preserves an ARRESTABLE patient; Code Blue manages an ARRESTED one. Many institutions allow family-activated RRT. Standing nurse-driven criteria empower the call without waiting for permission.

opts.text: TEAM ROLE ASSIGNMENT in the first 60 seconds is CORE charge RN/code leader skill: Primary RN (bedside), ICU Outreach RN (2nd IV + blood verifier), RT (airway), Intensivist/code leader (orders + source-control coordination), Charge RN (room runner + family + chain of command), Recorder (LVN — timeline + VS + MAR), Runner (CNA — blood retrieval + supplies + transport + family escort + BLS-ready)
opts.rat: CORRECT — clear role assignment prevents chaos + duplicated/missed tasks. Five Rights of Delegation still apply under pressure. Match each role to scope. The leader doesn't do every task — the leader assigns + supervises + escalates. Re-assess roles as situation evolves.

opts.text: RN-only tasks (NOT delegable to LVN or CNA even in emergency, California-aligned scope per CLAUDE.md §3): focused assessment of unstable patient, IV PUSH MEDS, BLOOD ADMINISTRATION + 2-RN verification + transfusion reaction monitoring, VASOPRESSOR initiation + titration, DIAGNOSTIC INTERPRETATION, care plan modification, TEACHING for new diagnoses, family clinical updates, SBAR escalation, INFORMED CONSENT witness, documentation oversight
opts.rat: CORRECT — RN scope boundaries. California LVN CANNOT push IV meds, hang blood/blood products, titrate vasopressors, interpret diagnostics, develop care plan, receive transfer report on unstable patient. Emergency doesn't change scope — clear role assignment within scope. RN retains accountability for delegated work.

opts.text: LVN-delegable tasks during RRT/MTP: serve as RECORDER (timeline + VS q15 min during MTP + MAR — data collection within scope; RN signs care plan elements), administer SCHEDULED PO/IM/SQ meds on stable adjacent patients, hang IV PIGGYBACK CRYSTALLOID (LR/NS — per cert), focused VS reassessment (RN interprets), hematoma inspection + report findings (no interpretation), reinforce family teaching, maintain sterile field, assist with positioning
opts.rat: CORRECT — LVN scope during code/RRT — substantial support work within scope. Recorder role is CRITICAL (legal record + ICU handoff). LVN frees RN for IV push + blood admin + vasopressor + assessment. Always escalate concerns to RN. RN supervises + retains accountability.

opts.text: CNA-delegable tasks during RRT/MTP: RUNNER (retrieve blood from blood bank with chain-of-custody slip — does NOT hang or verify; brings supplies + code cart + equipment; transports labs), CHEST COMPRESSIONS if patient codes (BLS-trained: depth 2-2.4 in, rate 100-120/min, full recoil, switch every 2 min), AED use (any BLS-trained), BVM ventilation (BLS), basic VS collection (RN/LVN interpret), patient positioning + transport, family escort + comfort + non-clinical info per RN guidance
opts.rat: CORRECT — CNA scope during code/RRT. The runner role is critical (delays in blood/supplies = patient harm). BLS-trained tasks (compressions, BVM, AED) within scope. CANNOT push meds, hang blood, interpret, assess, modify care plan. Scope does NOT change in emergency. Family clinical updates remain RN responsibility.

opts.text: MASSIVE TRANSFUSION PROTOCOL (MTP): balanced 1:1:1 ratio (PRBC:FFP:platelets) per PROPPR trial; O-negative emergency release until type-specific available; CALCIUM REPLACEMENT (citrate hypocalcemia — 1-2 g per 4 units PRBC); TXA 1 g IV × 10 min then 1 g × 8 hr (CRASH-2 — within 3 hr of hemorrhage); PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION SBP 80-90 until source control; WARM EVERYTHING (lethal triad); serial labs q15-30 min
opts.rat: CORRECT — MTP principles. Balanced ratios prevent dilutional coagulopathy from PRBC-only. Citrate in stored blood binds calcium → hypocalcemia → coagulopathy + arrhythmia. TXA antifibrinolytic (CRASH-2 best within 3 hr; possibly harmful >3 hr). Permissive hypotension avoids popping clot + diluting factors. Warming prevents lethal triad.

opts.text: 2-RN BLOOD VERIFICATION at bedside before each unit: 2 patient identifiers (name + DOB or MRN) against wristband AND blood product label, ABO/Rh compatibility, unit number + expiration + product integrity, both RNs sign; VS at baseline + 15 min after start + completion + q15 min during transfusion + 1 hr post; bedside RN x first 15 min of each unit for AHTR detection
opts.rat: CORRECT — 2-RN verification is the highest-yield safety check in transfusion. Wrong-blood-to-wrong-patient = sentinel event with ABO-incompatible mortality ~10%. First 15 min are highest AHTR risk — bedside RN presence. Distinguish reaction types (AHTR/TRALI/TACO/febrile non-hemolytic/allergic/bacterial) for appropriate intervention.

opts.text: LETHAL TRIAD (hypothermia + acidosis + coagulopathy) prevention/reversal: WARM EVERYTHING (Bair Hugger + warm fluids/blood via rapid infuser + warm humidified O2 + room temp + warm blankets + temp q15 min); treat acidosis at the source (perfusion + balanced products, NOT bicarbonate routinely); correct coagulopathy with balanced MTP + TXA + Ca + cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <150
opts.rat: CORRECT — lethal triad principles. Each component worsens the others. Cold platelets don't work; acidotic platelets don't work; coagulopathy → more bleeding → more shock → more acidosis. Bicarbonate generally NOT given unless pH <7.1 (controversial). LR or Plasma-Lyte preferred over NS (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis).

opts.text: Assign the LVN to push IV vasopressor boluses and hang the blood — in an emergency, scope rules are flexible to save lives
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG. Scope is NEVER flexible — even in emergency. California LVN CANNOT push IV meds, hang blood/blood products, titrate vasopressors. Doing so creates legal jeopardy, harms patient (LVN not trained), undermines team trust, violates licensure. The emergency INTENSIFIES the need for clear role assignment WITHIN scope. Bring additional RNs if needed.

opts.text: Aggressively resuscitate to a SBP target of ≥140 mmHg with 4-6 L normal saline bolus before considering blood products
opts.rat: ⚠️ DANGEROUSLY WRONG on multiple counts: (1) PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION target SBP 80-90 until source control (avoid popping clot + diluting factors); (2) BALANCED CRYSTALLOID (LR/Plasma-Lyte) preferred over NS (avoid hyperchloremic acidosis); (3) LIMIT CRYSTALLOID in favor of BLOOD PRODUCTS in hemorrhagic shock (over-crystalloid worsens dilutional coagulopathy); (4) blood should be activated SIMULTANEOUSLY with crystalloid, not after.

opts.text: Wait for type-specific crossmatched blood (typically 30-60 min) before transfusing — never use O-negative emergency release except in extreme situations
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. O-NEGATIVE EMERGENCY RELEASE is appropriate + life-saving when waiting for crossmatched blood would risk death. Standing protocol releases without crossmatch. Type & crossmatch SENT immediately, transition to type-specific products when available. Withholding blood while patient exsanguinates is harmful. MTP protocols release O-neg automatically on activation.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the charge RN priorities at this RRT call
steps.inst: Rank the charge RN priorities from #1 (HIGHEST) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Hemorrhagic Shock with active bleeding r/t post-arthroplasty deep wound bleed; Class III-IV shock with multi-organ hypoperfusion; ACTIVATE MTP + 2-RN blood verification + permissive hypotension + warming + serial labs
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Active hemorrhage with shock = imminent life threat. MTP activation + balanced 1:1:1 + calcium + TXA + warming + serial labs. Concurrent resuscitation + source control. Time-critical.

opts.text: Need for SOURCE CONTROL — the only definitive treatment; surgeon + IR consult immediately; decision IR embolization (less invasive, faster, identifies + treats) vs return-to-OR (definitive, required if IR fails or anatomy unfavorable); ICU bed reserved
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Resuscitation only buys time; source control is the only cure. Time-to-source-control is the most important modifiable factor. IR first-line if hemodynamically transportable; OR if unstable for transport or IR fails.

opts.text: TEAM ROLE ASSIGNMENT + Scope-aligned delegation: Primary RN (bedside), ICU Outreach RN (2nd IV + 2nd-RN blood verifier), RT (airway), Intensivist (orders + source-control coordination), Charge RN (room runner + family + chain of command), Recorder LVN (timeline), Runner CNA (blood retrieval + supplies)
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Clear role assignment in the first 60 seconds is the CORE charge RN skill. Without it, chaos + duplicated/missed tasks. Five Rights of Delegation still apply under pressure. Match each role to scope; never assign outside scope even in emergency.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION + SBAR HANDOFF: real-time timeline by LVN recorder; all interventions + times + medications + blood products + lab trends + family communication + provider notifications; SBAR handoff to IR + ICU teams; legal record + quality improvement + clinical continuity
opts.rat: #4 — Important throughout, lower acute priority than physiologic threats but essential for legal record + quality improvement + clinical handoff. Recorder role critical. Reconstruct timeline immediately post-event if real-time documentation impossible.

opts.text: FAMILY COMMUNICATION + SUPPORT: designated family liaison provides honest updates every 15-30 min in private family room within unit; chaplain + social work; family presence during resuscitation per policy + preference; informed consent witness for procedures
opts.rat: #5 — Important but lowest acute priority. Family-centered care during code/RRT. Designated liaison ensures consistent updates without distracting bedside team. Honest simple language. Chaplain + social work activation. Informed consent obtained by provider; RN witnesses + reinforces.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Who does each task — RN, LVN, CNA, or RT/Provider?
steps.inst: For EACH task, classify which team member is appropriate.

opts.text: Focused rapid assessment of unstable patient (ABCs, neuro, hematoma, perfusion); IV push medications (calcium gluconate, fentanyl, vasopressor boluses); vasopressor initiation + titration (norepinephrine drip)
opts.rat: RN-only. Assessment of unstable patient + IV push + vasopressor initiation/titration are RN-retained. Cannot be delegated to LVN or CNA. California LVN scope restriction. Primary RN handles at bedside.

opts.text: Blood product administration: 2-RN bedside verification (patient identifiers vs wristband + blood label, ABO/Rh compatibility, unit number, expiration, product integrity); start transfusion via Level-1 rapid infuser; transfusion reaction monitoring (VS at baseline + 15 min + completion + q15 min); bedside x first 15 min for AHTR detection
opts.rat: RN-only. Blood administration + 2-RN verification + transfusion reaction monitoring are RN-retained (California LVN cannot administer blood/blood products per BVNPT). Wrong-blood-to-wrong-patient is a sentinel event with ABO-incompatible mortality ~10%.

opts.text: Real-time recorder role: timeline of events (RRT call, arrivals, role assignments, MTP activation, every intervention with time); VS q5 min during active resuscitation, q15 min during MTP/stabilization; medication MAR (RN/provider order; recorder documents); fluid I&O; lab results as they return
opts.rat: Delegable to LVN. Documentation of timeline + VS + MAR is within LVN scope (data collection). RN reviews + signs care plan elements + interpretive notes. LVN escalates concerns to RN. Recorder role is critical (legal record + handoff).

opts.text: Retrieve MTP cooler from blood bank with chain-of-custody slip; bring supplies + code cart + equipment from supply room; transport labs to lab; bring paged equipment (rapid infuser, warmer, central line kit)
opts.rat: Delegable to CNA. Runner role — retrieving + transporting items is within CNA scope. CNA does NOT hang or verify blood (RN does at bedside). Delays in supplies or blood = patient harm — runner role is critical.

opts.text: Diagnostic interpretation: 12-lead ECG, ABG, CBC, coags, fibrinogen, ionized Ca, lactate, type & cross results; clinical synthesis driving treatment decisions
opts.rat: RN-only. Diagnostic interpretation requires clinical judgment + is RN-retained. LVN cannot interpret diagnostic results. Provider/intensivist may also interpret + order — but the bedside RN integrates findings + acts.

opts.text: Administer scheduled PO/IM/SQ medications to STABLE adjacent patients on the unit (charge RN's other assigned patients) while resuscitation in progress; document MAR
opts.rat: Delegable to LVN. Medication administration on stable patients within LVN scope (PO/IM/SQ + IV piggyback per cert). Reassigning LVN workload as priorities shift is appropriate. RN supervises + checks in on stable patients.

opts.text: Chest compressions if patient progresses to arrest (BLS-trained: depth 2-2.4 in adult, rate 100-120/min, full recoil, switch every 2 min to prevent fatigue); BVM ventilation per BLS; AED use
opts.rat: Delegable to CNA. BLS-trained tasks (compressions, BVM, AED) are within CNA scope. ANY BLS-trained provider can perform these — not RN-only. ACLS-trained provider runs the code (rhythm interpretation, manual defibrillation, drug push, intubation).

opts.text: Family communication: clinical updates on patient status + plan + disposition (IR vs OR vs ICU); informed consent witness for IR/OR procedures (provider obtains; RN witnesses + reinforces); chaplain + social work coordination
opts.rat: RN-only for clinical updates + consent witness + complex family conversations. CNA can escort family + provide comfort items + basic non-clinical information (e.g., 'I'll get you a chair and bring an update from the nurse in 10 minutes') but NOT clinical updates. Chaplain + social work coordination is RN-led.

opts.text: Escort family to private family room within unit (accessible for updates — not removed from facility); provide chairs + water + comfort items; basic non-clinical information ('The team is taking very good care of your wife. The nurse will be in with an update in a few minutes')
opts.rat: Delegable to CNA. Family escort + comfort items + basic non-clinical reassurance within CNA scope. CNA does NOT provide clinical updates (RN does). Designated family-support staff (chaplain, social work) can also support — coordinate roles.

opts.text: SBAR escalation to surgeon + interventional radiology + ICU + blood bank + chain of command (charge RN → nurse manager → nursing supervisor → medical director) if any provider non-responsive or safety concerns
opts.rat: RN-only. Escalation + SBAR to providers + chain of command activation requires clinical judgment + RN authority. LVN can communicate observations to RN but does not escalate to providers independently. Charge RN owns chain of command activation.

opts.text: Assign LVN to push IV vasopressor boluses and hang the first PRBC unit to save time during the active resuscitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ SCOPE VIOLATION if delegated to LVN. California LVN CANNOT push IV meds or hang blood/blood products even in emergency. Bring additional RNs if needed (additional rapid response staff, ICU outreach, float pool RN). NEVER delegate outside scope — creates legal jeopardy + patient harm + team trust erosion.

opts.text: Maintain sterile field for central line/arterial line placement; assist with patient positioning (Trendelenburg controversial — generally avoid in shock; semi-Fowler for respiratory distress); maintain abductor pillow + hip precautions during repositioning
opts.rat: Delegable to LVN. Sterile field maintenance + assist with positioning within LVN scope. RN places lines (or provider does). LVN reinforces hip precautions previously taught. RN/provider makes positioning decisions (Trendelenburg generally avoided in shock per current evidence).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: FIRST action sequence at the RRT call
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the charge RN should implement these actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: ASSIGN ROLES within 60 seconds of arrival: Primary RN bedside (assessment + IV push + vasopressor), ICU Outreach RN (2nd large-bore IV + 2nd-RN blood verifier), RT (airway/O2/ABG), Intensivist (code leader + orders), Charge RN — me (room runner + family + chain of command + documentation oversight), Recorder LVN (timeline + VS + MAR), Runner CNA (blood retrieval + supplies + transport + family escort + BLS-ready)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Clear role assignment in the first 60 seconds is the CORE charge RN/code leader skill. Without it, chaos + duplicated/missed tasks. Five Rights of Delegation still apply under pressure. Match each role to scope. The leader assigns + supervises.

opts.text: ACTIVATE MTP via blood bank call: 'Activate MTP for [patient name + MRN] — hemorrhagic shock from post-op hip arthroplasty bleed; first cooler stat; O-negative emergency release until type-specific available'; type & crossmatch SENT; prepare for 2-RN bedside verification; start TXA 1 g IV × 10 min + calcium gluconate per 4 units PRBC + warming measures (Bair Hugger + warm fluids/blood via rapid infuser)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. MTP activation is the primary intervention for hemorrhagic shock. Balanced 1:1:1 + calcium + TXA + warming + permissive hypotension + serial labs. Time-critical. Communicate clearly to blood bank for stat cooler delivery.

opts.text: COORDINATE SOURCE CONTROL: STAT surgeon + interventional radiology consult; intensivist + surgeon + IR conference for decision (IR embolization first-line if hemodynamically transportable, OR if too unstable or IR fails); ICU bed reserved post-procedure
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Source control is the only definitive treatment. Resuscitation only buys time. Time-to-source-control is the most important modifiable factor. IR + OR + ICU coordination in parallel with resuscitation.

opts.text: FAMILY COMMUNICATION + SUPPORT: husband + adult daughter escorted by CNA to private family room within unit (accessible for updates — not removed from facility); designated family liaison (charge RN or social worker) provides honest updates every 15-30 min; chaplain + social work paged; informed consent for IR/OR procedures by provider (RN witnesses)
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Family-centered care during code/RRT. Designated liaison ensures consistent updates without distracting bedside team. Honest simple language. Chaplain + social work activation. Informed consent obtained by provider; RN witnesses + reinforces.

opts.text: DOCUMENTATION + HANDOFF: real-time recorder (LVN) documents timeline + interventions + medications + blood products + lab trends + family communication + provider notifications; SBAR handoff to IR + ICU teams; debrief planned post-event for team learning + quality improvement
opts.rat: #5 — Continuous + final. Documentation is essential for legal record + quality improvement + clinical handoff. Recorder role critical. SBAR handoff standardizes transitions. Debriefing post-event captures lessons learned + supports team emotional well-being.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: 1015 — IR embolization successful (superior gluteal artery branch embolized with coil); SBP 102/64, HR 108, MAP 76, lactate 3.2 (down from 5.8), Hgb 9.2 (post 4 units PRBC + 4 units FFP + 1 platelets); transferred to ICU for continued monitoring; warming continued; transfusion reaction surveillance ongoing
opts.rat: Successful source control + balanced resuscitation. Hemodynamic improvement, lactate clearance, Hgb stabilization. ICU disposition for continued monitoring. Source control achieved — resuscitation can de-escalate (vasopressor weaning, transition to standard MAP targets ≥65, complete MTP if remaining transfusions needed).

opts.text: ICU Day 2 — extubated; off vasopressors; Hgb 8.4 stable; coag panel normalized; lactate 1.2; tolerating clears; PT/OT bedside; family at bedside; surgical team rounding; plan for transfer to step-down today + return to ortho floor in 2-3 days
opts.rat: Excellent post-source-control recovery. Standard ICU de-escalation. Multidisciplinary care. Family engagement. Anticipate transfer to step-down → floor → rehab. Continued ortho follow-up for hip recovery. Surgical site monitoring.

opts.text: Discharge Day 10 — discharged to acute rehab for continued PT + wound care; ambulating with walker + hip precautions reinforced; outpatient ortho + PCP + hematology (for transfusion-related follow-up) follow-up; family supportive; functional recovery expected over 6-12 weeks
opts.rat: Successful recovery trajectory. Acute rehab for functional restoration. Multidisciplinary outpatient follow-up. Long-term hip arthroplasty recovery timeline (6-12 weeks for return to most activities). Transfusion-related follow-up (delayed transfusion reactions, hemovigilance reporting completed).

opts.text: 0945 — During PRBC unit 2 transfusion, patient develops new fever 38.8°C, chills, flank pain, dark urine, BP drops to 70/40, oozing from IV site — ACUTE HEMOLYTIC TRANSFUSION REACTION (AHTR); STOP transfusion, maintain IV with NS via NEW tubing, notify provider + blood bank, return blood + tubing + post-transfusion samples to blood bank; supportive care + diuretics for hemoglobinuria + monitoring for DIC
opts.rat: ⚠️ AHTR — sentinel transfusion reaction (mortality ~10% for ABO-incompatible). STOP transfusion immediately. Maintain IV access with NS via NEW tubing (do NOT continue suspected blood through same tubing). Treat hypotension + hemoglobinuria + monitor for DIC + AKI. Hemovigilance reporting. Root cause analysis for verification process.

opts.text: 1000 — During resuscitation, LVN reports she went ahead and hung the second PRBC unit because RN was busy with vasopressor; patient now showing rigor + hypotension + bronchospasm — possible AHTR vs anaphylactic transfusion reaction; LVN admits not performing 2-RN verification because of urgency
opts.rat: ⚠️ SCOPE VIOLATION + POTENTIAL SENTINEL EVENT. California LVN cannot administer blood/blood products. 2-RN verification skipped = increased wrong-blood-to-patient risk. STOP transfusion, treat reaction (STOP + EPI if anaphylaxis features, supportive care + diuretics if hemolysis), return blood + samples, NOTIFY provider + blood bank + risk management + nurse manager. Address scope violation respectfully + reinforce education; Just Culture focus on system process (was RN support inadequate? was role assignment clear?) over individual blame. Document. Hemovigilance reporting. Possible sentinel event review.

opts.text: 0955 — Patient progresses to cardiopulmonary arrest despite ongoing resuscitation; pulse lost; CODE BLUE activated; CNA initiates chest compressions per BLS training (depth 2-2.4 in, rate 100-120/min, full recoil, switch every 2 min); intensivist + anesthesia at bedside; emergent return to OR for open exploration after ROSC + ACLS
opts.rat: Progression from RRT (deterioration) to CODE BLUE (arrest). CNA chest compressions are appropriate (BLS-trained). ACLS algorithm by intensivist + team. Emergent OR for open surgical exploration after ROSC (or sometimes during arrest if surgeon present). Family communication during code per institutional FPDR policy. Document timeline.

opts.text: 1130 — Bedside team debrief completed: reviewed timeline, decision points, what went well (clear role assignment, timely MTP, communication), what could improve (recorder needed an additional pen + paper supply, family room phone broken, blood bank delivery took 12 min — goal <10), quality improvement note submitted for unit + system review; emotional support offered to team via EAP + peer support
opts.rat: Standard post-event debriefing. Just Culture focus on system + process improvement rather than individual blame. Captures lessons learned. Quality improvement opportunities identified. Emotional support for team (high-acuity events take toll). Document debrief + action items + ownership for follow-up.

opts.text: 1300 — Husband expresses gratitude for clear communication throughout, says he felt 'kept in the loop' even during the most intense moments; daughter asks for clarification on what happened + outlook; charge RN provides honest summary + facilitates family meeting with surgeon/intensivist + chaplain support
opts.rat: Successful family-centered care during crisis. Honest communication + accessibility builds trust. Family meeting with attending providers for clinical detail + outlook. Chaplain support. Document family meetings + understanding. Standard practice for post-resuscitation family integration.

opts.text: ICU Day 1 — develops new bilateral pulmonary infiltrates + hypoxia (PaO2/FiO2 ratio 180) within 4 hr of large-volume transfusion; afebrile; no signs of volume overload (CVP normal, no JVP, no edema); TRANSFUSION-RELATED ACUTE LUNG INJURY (TRALI) diagnosed; supportive care with lung-protective ventilation; donor-specific antibody workup
opts.rat: TRALI — non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema within 6 hr of transfusion. Supportive care with lung-protective ventilation (tidal volume 6 mL/kg PBW, plateau pressure <30, permissive hypercapnia). Diuretics NOT helpful (not volume overload). Donor-specific antibody workup. Resolves over days. Distinguishable from TACO (TACO = volume overload + diuretics helpful; TRALI = non-cardiogenic + supportive only).

opts.text: 1500 — Charge RN completes shift handoff via SBAR to night charge; ortho floor census + acuity reviewed; one new admission expected later; LVN + CNA work reviewed (Just Culture conversation about MTP scope incident planned with nurse manager next business day); event documentation complete; quality improvement note submitted; debrief findings shared
opts.rat: Standard end-of-shift charge RN workflow. SBAR handoff. Just Culture conversation for scope incident (focus on system improvement, education, not individual blame). Documentation complete. Quality improvement note for unit-level + system-level learning. Continuity of care maintained.
